pageDesc: Contracts where the awarded price significantly exceeds sector and category norms. IQR-based statistical detection normalized per sector and year baseline — extreme overpricing flags contracts above Q3 + 3× IQR.
totalAnomaliesDetail: Total flagged contracts across all sectors
totalFlaggedDetail: Total MXN value of anomalous contracts
extremeConfidenceDetail: Avg confidence for extreme overpricing type
sectorMapDesc: Price anomaly count by federal sector — sorted by volume
workbenchDesc: Sortable, filterable table of all price anomaly hypotheses. Click a row to inspect sector baseline context. Use the action buttons to mark hypotheses valid or invalid.
noResults: No price anomaly contracts match the current filters.
baselinesSubtitle: IQR thresholds used for anomaly detection (click to expand)
baselinesDesc: Statistical distribution of contract values per sector. The upper fence (Q3 + 1.5× IQR) is the outlier threshold; the extreme fence (Q3 + 3× IQR) triggers the extreme overpricing flag.
headline: The Secret Market: Prices That Don't Add Up
lede: Statistical analysis of 3.05 million contracts reveals that {{countLabel}} were procured at prices exceeding 3 times the market median. In health and technology, overpricing is most frequent. The cumulative value of these anomalous contracts totals {{total}}.
errorMessage: Could not load price information. Please try again later.
moreThan: more expensive than the sector median
methodologyPara1: Each contract is compared against the price distribution of its sector and year using z-scores. A price z-score above 3 means the amount exceeds 3 standard deviations above the sector mean, placing it in the top 0.1% of the distribution.
methodologyPara2: Additionally, the system uses the interquartile range (IQR) method to detect outliers. The alert threshold is set at Q3 + 1.5× IQR (statistical outlier) and Q3 + 3.0× IQR (extreme overpricing).
methodologyPara3: Measures the variance of a vendor's contract amounts relative to sector norms. High volatility suggests inconsistent pricing or selective manipulation. It is the strongest predictor in the v6.5 risk model (coefficient +0.53).
methodologyDisclaimer: Risk scores are statistical indicators that measure similarity to documented corruption patterns. A high score does not constitute proof of irregularity.
zThresholdHint: Higher values = only the most extreme cases
humanImpactPara: In the health sector, systematic overpricing in drug and medical equipment contracts means the government could have purchased significantly more supplies with the same budget. This is the real cost of overpricing:
avgZDesc: Standard deviations above the sector mean in contracts with detected anomalous prices. Contracts:
extremeCasesSubtitle: Contracts with the greatest deviation from their sector and year median. A factor of 8.3× means the government paid 8.3 times the typical price.
anomalousContractsByYear: Contracts with anomalous pricing by year (2015–2025)
contractsAwardedAtAnomalousPrice: Contracts awarded at statistically anomalous prices
kpiLede: {{n}} federal contracts worth {{value}} were awarded at statistically anomalous prices — defined as {{z}} standard deviations above the sector market median.
overpricingTooltipBody: Calculation: total_value × (1 − 1/avg_z) = {{value}} × {{pct}}%. This is a statistical approximation, not a confirmed amount.
noAnomaliesAtThreshold: No price anomalies found at the current threshold. Try lowering the minimum Z-score.
